steps.description: Face the front end of the Ø65 cast iron bar to establish a clean Z-zero reference point.
Rough turn the outer diameter of the exposed stock from Ø65 mm down to Ø61 mm for a length of approximately 115 mm.
Perform step turning from the front face: turn Ø20 mm for 10 mm length, then Ø35 mm for 15 mm length, followed by Ø30 mm for 40 mm length.
Continue step turning: turn Ø45 mm for 20 mm length, Ø55 mm for 20 mm length, and the final Ø45 mm section for 10 mm length.
Apply a 2 mm chamfer at the transition between the Ø20 and Ø35 sections, and a 1 mm chamfer at the transition between the Ø30 and Ø45 sections.
Flip the part in the chuck, clamping on the finished Ø45 or Ø55 sections using soft jaws. Face the second end to achieve the total part length of 150 mm.
Turn the remaining Ø65 mm stock down to the final Ø60 mm diameter for the remaining 35 mm length.

reasoning: The manufacturing process is designed for a multi-stepped cylindrical part made of cast iron. The raw stock is Ø65 mm x 150 mm, which matches the final part's total length, requiring precise facing to maintain dimensions. The part features several diameter changes (Ø20, Ø35, Ø30, Ø45, Ø55, Ø45, Ø60) and specific chamfers. My reasoning follows a two-setup approach. Setup 1 focuses on machining the complex right-hand side of the drawing (Ø20 through the second Ø45 section) while clamping on the Ø65 stock. This allows for continuous turning of the nested diameters and precise chamfering. Setup 2 involves flipping the part to finish the Ø60 section and face the part to the exact 150 mm length. Cast iron is brittle and requires moderate speeds; I've selected RPMs that balance surface finish with tool life, using higher speeds for smaller diameters and finishing passes.
step_by_step_plan:
1. Load the Ø65 mm x 150 mm cast iron bar into the CNC lathe chuck, ensuring at least 120 mm is protruding.
2. Use the Facing Tool at 800 RPM to face the end of the bar, setting this as the Z0 coordinate.
3. Use a Rough Turning Tool to reduce the stock diameter to Ø61 mm for a length of 115 mm to remove the outer casting skin.
4. Switch to a Finishing Turning Tool. Starting from Z0, turn the Ø20 mm section to a length of 10 mm. 
5. Proceed to turn the Ø35 mm section for the next 15 mm (Z-10 to Z-25).
6. Turn the Ø30 mm section for the next 40 mm (Z-25 to Z-65).
7. Turn the Ø45 mm section for the next 20 mm (Z-65 to Z-85).
8. Turn the Ø55 mm section for the next 20 mm (Z-85 to Z-105).
9. Turn the final Ø45 mm section for the next 10 mm (Z-105 to Z-115).
10. Use the Chamfering Tool to create a 2 mm x 45° chamfer on the edge of the Ø35 shoulder and a 1 mm x 45° chamfer on the edge of the first Ø45 shoulder.
11. Unclamp the part, flip it, and clamp on the Ø55 or Ø45 section using soft jaws to protect the finish.
12. Face the remaining end to bring the total part length to exactly 150 mm.
13. Turn the remaining Ø65 mm stock down to Ø60 mm for the final 35 mm length of the part.
14. Inspect all diameters and lengths against the engineering drawing for quality assurance.
